contents:
Brief Introduction In August 2024, I joined the TEDxNational Taiwan University organizing team as the Director of Public Relations.
Our annual exhibition, the first large-scale event we have hosted, took place from December 2 to December 6, 2024, at National Taiwan University.
About the Annual Exhibition The annual exhibition is the most significant event of TEDxNational Taiwan University during the first semester. Its main goal is to present the concepts and issues we aim to explore this year through an engaging exhibition.
The theme of this year’s annual exhibition “Life Playlist” seeks to integrate the concept of a playlist with the annual theme “Pause”. It aims to guide attendees in stepping away from their daily routines to reflect on and reconsider the issues in their lives from a new perspective.
My Role I led the Public Relations team in establishing business collaborations with companies.
Since our annual exhibition was free to attend, we lacked additional revenue to support its organization. Therefore, the Public Relations team worked closely with various companies to secure sufficient funding for the event.
Thanks to the generous support from our partners, we obtained over NT$100,000 in sponsorships, enabling the successful execution of the exhibition, which attracted over 1,500 visitors.
Reflections First and foremost, I am deeply grateful to all the companies that supported our nonprofit event. Without their help, it would have been challenging to organize this exhibition.
Additionally, I want to thank our entire organizing team. Each member put their utmost effort into their respective responsibilities, and it was our teamwork that made this event such a success.
Lastly, I want to thank every attendee who visited the exhibition. Your participation gave us tremendous support, and many expressed their gratitude and encouragement to the team, which serves as a powerful motivation for us to keep striving forward.
title: 2024 TEDxNational Taiwan University Annual Exhibition

contents:
Due to some information in this project being confidential, the complete source code will not be disclosed.
Background of the Issue My internship company (hereinafter referred to as Company A) has outsourced its cleaning and security services to two different companies: Company B for cleaning and Company C for security. Both the cleaning staff and security personnel work different shifts, such as morning, evening, or night, with separate schedules for weekdays and weekends.
Company A’s management department’s partial daily workflow is shown in the diagram below:
Due to the different and complex shifts for cleaning and security personnel, calculating attendance status daily is very time-consuming.
Given the high level of repetition in the aforementioned calculation process and the frequent errors in manual calculations, I will use programming languages to automate it.
Primary Tools Used This project primarily uses Python as the main programming language, utilizing the Datetime module for time format conversions and calculations, and the Pandas module for handling interactions between Excel, .CSV files, and data operations.
Main Workflow The main process will be divided into the following steps:
Access raw data Shift data configuration Determine the clock-in and clock-out times for each record Calculate attendance status Output calculation results Step1: Access raw data The .CSV file format of the raw data is similar to the image below.
Since employees at Company B frequently work in shifts for others, the raw data does not label the shift type for Company B’s records.
We first import the necessary “pandas” and “datetime” modules, then set the “today_str” variable based on the date input by the user, and use “today_str” to read the corresponding raw data ".csv" file.
import pandas as pd import datetime today_str = input("Date to be checked:") # Format: YYYY/MM/DD today_data = pd.read_csv(today_str + ".csv", encoding="ansi") Step2: Shift data configuration We first define the time format conversion functions from the “datetime” module to simplify the code for later use.
The “interval_transformation” function will use the value of “controller” to determine which time to use as the reference within a flexible time period, and then convert that time string into a time format.
def time_transformation(time_str): return datetime.datetime.strptime(time_str, "%H:%M:%S") def date_transformation(date_str): return datetime.datetime.strptime(date_str, "%Y/%m/%d %H:%M:%S") def str_transformation(time): return time.strftime("%Y/%m/%d %H:%M:%S") def time_transformation2(timedelta): return timedelta.strftime("%Y/%m/%d %H:%M:%S") """ The value of controller will determine which time to use as the reference for a flexible time period. If the value is 0, the earlier time is used as the reference; if the value is 1, the later time is used as the reference. """ def interval_transformation(interval_str, controller): # interval_str format: "hh:mm:ss-hh:mm:ss" or "hh:mm:ss" if "-" not in interval_str: return date_transformation(today_str + " " + interval_str) else: return date_transformation(today_str + " " + (interval_str.split("-"))[controller]) Create a generic shift class and store the attributes specific to each shift type within it.
""" in_time = shift clock-in time out_time = shift clock-out time wrokers = list of all employees for that shift minimum_hours = minimum working hours for that shift weekday_required = minimum number of people required for that shift on weekdays weekend_required = minimum number of people required for that shift on weekends shift_name = name of the shift replacement = dictionary of individuals who may be substitutes for that shift """ class shift: def __init__(self, time1, time2, people, minimum_hour, weekday, weekend, name, replace): self.in_time = interval_transformation(interval_str=time1, controller=1) self.out_time = interval_transformation(interval_str=time2, controller=0) self.workers = people self.minimum_hours = minimum_hour self.weekday_required = weekday self.weekend_required = weekend self.attendance = 0 self.shift_name = name self.replacement = replace # Store the attributes for each shift in their respective data. exmaple_shift = shift(time1="07:30:59-08:30:59", time2="16:30:00-17:30:00", minimum_hour = 9 weekday=15, weekend=3, name="Company_B_morning", replace=[]) Step3: Determine the clock-in and clock-out times for each record We define a function called “employee_classification” to store employee attendance data by time into a shift-based dictionary. Since employees often have issues with incorrect card swipes, resulting in multiple attendance records for the same swipe, we ignore duplicate attendance data for the same person within a five-minute interval.
# Define a function to store employee attendance data by time into a shift-based dictionary. def employee_classification(shift_dict, name, time): if name not in shift_dict.keys(): shift[name] = [time] elif (date_transformation(time) - date_transformation(shift_dict[name][-1]) >= datetime.datetime(minutes=5)): # Ignore data for the same person within a five-minute interval. shift_dict[name].append(time) Then we use a for loop to iterate through each attendance record in the raw data. As mentioned earlier, employees at Company B frequently have shift substitutions, and the original data does not label shifts. Therefore, we cannot categorize Company B’s data by shift at this point; instead, we will use other functions later to perform the calculations for shift substitutions.
B_employees = {} # A dictionary for recording attendance data for Company B employees. C_morning = {} # A dictionary for recording attendance data for Company C employees. for index, row in today_data.iterrows(): # for loop to iterate through each attendance record. name_str = str(row.iloc[0]) time_str = str(row.iloc[1]) shift_str = str(row.iloc[3]) if today_str in time_str: # Only keep today's data. if "exampleB" in name_str: # Check if it belongs to Company B. employee_classification(shift_dict=B_employees, name=name_str, time=time_str) if "exampleC" in name_str: # Check if it belongs to Company C. if "morning" in shift_str: employee_classification(shift_dict=C_morning, name=name_str, time=time_str) Under normal circumstances, an employee would swipe their card once when entering the company and once when leaving, so theoretically, their attendance record should be an even number. We define the “even_check” function to verify whether the attendance count is even and use this function to verify the dictionaries for each different shift.
# Check if the number of attendance instances is even. def even_check(people_dict): for people in people_dict.keys(): if len(people_dict[people]) % 2 != 0: error_people[people] = people_dict[people] for people in error_people.keys(): if people in people_dict.keys(): del people_dict[people] Step4: Calculate attendance status for each shift We define a function to subtract the shift working hours from the employee’s attendance periods to avoid counting the employee’s attendance multiple times.
""" Define a function to subtract the shift working hours from the employee attendance period. time_list represents the employee's attendance periods, start is the shift start time, and end is the shift end time. """ def clear(time_list, start, end): counter = 0 while (start < date_transformation(time_list[2 * counter]) or end > date_transformation(time_list[2 * counter + 1])): counter += 1 if counter > (len(time_list) / 2 - 1): break if counter <= (len(time_list) / 2 - 1): time_list.insert((2 * counter + 1), str_transformation(start)) time_list.insert((2 * counter + 2), str_transformation(end)) return time_list Since employees who assist with substitute shifts sometimes use a card named “Substitute” for clocking in and sometimes use their own cards, we cannot directly determine whether an employee is a substitute based on the card name used for clocking in. So, we design a process to calculate the number of employees present while also considering substitute shifts.
As shown in the image below, the orange line represents the working hours of a shift, the blue line represents employee D’s attendance period, and the green line represents employee E’s attendance period. Assume that employee E is assisting employee D by covering the entire shift’s working hours.
Since employee D’s clock-out time is earlier than the shift end time, we set employee D’s clock-out time as “boundary point X.” Similarly, because employee E’s clock-in time is later than the shift start time, we set employee E’s clock-in time as “boundary point Y” to segment the time periods.
Then we can create segments A, B, and C by pairing the shift start time, shift end time, and each of the boundary points with their adjacent neighbors. At this point, we just need to check whether the number of employees present in each segment A, B and C is greater than 1 to determine if there are employees present throughout the entire shift working hours.
We achieve the creation of boundary points by defining the “ordering” function.
# Define a function to insert a specific time into a time-sorted list containing different times. def ordering(inserted_time, time_list): counter = 1 while date_transformation(inserted_time) > date_transformation(time_list[counter]): counter += 1 if counter >= len(time_list): break time_list.insert(counter, inserted_time) Next, we apply the “ordering” function within the new “boundary_check” function to complete the entire verification process.
""" Define a function to check shift substitutions and the number of employees present, and return [number of employees present, substitute01, substitute02...] start_str = shift start time string end_str = shift end time string people_dict = a dictionary that records the clock-in and clock-out times of employees and substitutes. """ def boundary_check(start_str, end_str, people_dict): shift_start = date_transformation(start_str) shift_end = date_transformation(end_str) time_line = [shift_start, shift_end] result_list = [] # Use a for loop to apply the "ordering" function to all employees in the dictionary to derive the boundary points. for people1 in people_dict.keys(): for k in range(len(people_dict[people1]) // 2): in_time1 = date_transformation(people_dict[people1][2 * k]) out_time1 = date_trnasformation(people_dict[people1][2 * k + 1]) if (in_time1 > shift_start and in_time1 < shift_end and people_dict[people1][2 * k] not in time_line): ordering(inserted_time=people_dict[people1][2 * k], time_list=time_line) if (out_time1 < shift_end and out_time1 > shift_start and people_dict[people1][2 * k + 1] not in time_line): ordering(inserted_time=people_dict[people1][2 * k + 1], time_list=time_line) # Check the number of employees present in each segment between adjacent boundary points. for i in range(len(time_line) - 1): start = date_transformation(time_line[i]) end = date_transformation(time_line[i + 1]) counter = 0 # Record the number of employees present in the current segment. replace = [] # A list that stores individuals who successfully substituted using a "Substitute" card. for people2 in people_dict.keys(): for j in range(len(people_dict[people2]) // 2): in_time2 = date_transformation(people_dict[people2][2 * j]) out_time2 = date_transformation(people_dict[people2][2 * k + 1]) if in_time2 <= start and out_time2 >= end: counter += 1 if "Substitue" in people2 and people2 not in replace: replace.append(people2) result_list.append(counter) minimum_attend = [float("inf")] for result in result_list: if result < minimum_attend[0]: minimum_attend = [result] return minimum_attend + replace Finally, we can use the ".weekday()" method to determine the day of the week for the date being checked, and apply the various check functions mentioned earlier according to the different parameters for weekdays and weekends for each shift.
if date_transformation(today_str + " " + "06:00:00").weekday() <= 5: Step5: Output calculation results In the final part, we need to first create a DataFrame format to facilitate the subsequent generation of the Excel file.
# Create a dictionary for the DataFrame to facilitate the final file output. result = {today_str:[], "Check_results":[]} Next, we define the “basic_generate” function to handle the addition of data for file generation. We then use this function to construct the subsequent “generate” and “result_generate” functions, which will be used to produce a complete attendance status for a shift.
# Define a function to add the content to be produced into the DataFrame. def basic_generate(left_list, right_list, left_arg, right_arg): left_list.append(left_arg) right_list.append(right_arg) # Use the previously defined "basic_generate" function to fully generate the attendance status for a shift. def result_generate(name, required, attend, replacement): basic_generate(left_list=result[today_str], right_list=result["Check_results"], left_arg=" ", right_arg=" ") def generate(status): basic_generate(left_list=result[today_str], right_list=result["Check_results"], left_arg=name, right_arg=status) basic_generate(left_list=result[today_str], right_list=result["Check_results"], left_arg="Expected_attendees", right_arg=required) basic_generate(left_list=result[today_str], right_list=result["Check_results"], left_arg="Actual_attendees", right_arg=attend) if required <= attend: generate("Attendance_normal") if len(replacement) > 0: for i in replacement: basic_generate(left_list=result[today_str], right_list=result["Check_results"], left_arg="Substitute employee", right_arg=i) else: generate("Attendance_abnormal") if len(replacement) > 0: for i in replacement: basic_generate(left_list=result[today_str], right_list=result["Check_results"], left_arg="Substitute employee", right_arg=i) Finally, use the functions to add the complete attendance status for each shift to the DataFrame, and then generate the Excel file from the DataFrame.
output_excel = pd.DataFrame(result) output_excel.to_excel("output.xlsx", index=False) The attendance report generated after execution will be similar to the image below:
Future Benefits Firstly, automation resolves the issue of calculation errors that occur during manual operations, mitigating the additional risks and subsequent costs associated with human mistakes.
Secondly, it reduces daily labor costs. According to the management department, the manual calculation process previously required about 30 minutes each day for data verification and comparison, so automation can effectively save labor costs associated with repetitive tasks.
title: Automating the Attendance System

contents:
Introduction to the BA Summer Camp “The BA Summer Camp” stands for “the Business Administration Summer Camp”, and it is specifically organized for Taiwanese high school students to learn about business administration and related fields.
The Camp’s Curriculum and Content As mentioned earlier, our primary goal is to introduce high school students to the field of business administration. Therefore, the curriculum focuses on the five key areas of business administration:
Production Marketing Human Resources Research and Development Finance In terms of professional courses, we invite industry experts with practical experience to teach the specialized classes. Additionally, we design business-related games ourselves to engage students and introduce them to business administration concepts in an interactive way.
The Reasons I Volunteer as a Teacher The first reason is gratitude and contentment. I feel that I have received a lot of help from many people throughout my educational journey, including teachers, classmates, mentors, and parents. Therefore, I hope to give back to students within my capacity.
Secondly, I am a former participant of this camp. The camp not only introduced me to the field of business administration but also allowed me to make many good friends from different schools. Therefore, I believe this camp is very worthwhile and should continue to be supported and held.
My Role at the Camp I am responsible for leading a particular team composed of high school students from various cities across Taiwan. In addition to leading these students through the courses, I also need to foster team spirit and address various questions about university and business administration.
Due to the uniqueness of my interests and academic background, “Finding Your Own Position” resonates deeply with me. As a result, I am also a speaker on this topic, responsible for sharing how to discover one’s own interests and role, with the goal of inspiring students who feel uncertain about their future.
Summary I am very pleased to have a group of like-minded university students volunteering with me to support the organization of this camp. I highly recommend that friends who share similar values join in supporting this camp and encourage high school students to participate in similar activities to explore their interests.
title: Introduction to the BA Summer Camp

contents:
In the following article, we use “NTU BA” to refer to the department of Business Administration at National Taiwan University.
A Brief Introduction to NTU BA NTU BA is affiliated with the College of Management at National Taiwan University. Our curriculum includes foundational business courses such as calculus, statistics, economics, accounting, and financial management. We also offer unique courses compared to other departments in the College of Management, such as marketing management, strategic management, and management science model. Most notably, we offer supply chain courses** that are less common in other universities’ BA departments, such as optimization methods, supply chain management, and logistics management.
Skills development The required credits for core courses in NTU BA are fewer compared to most other departments, allowing us enough time to develop various specialties. Furthermore, our department offers four applied modules for study, including:
Marketing Data Analytics Human Resources Technology Management However, many students also focus on fields such as computer sciences, industrial engineering, mathematics, or other areas.
Daily life in NTU BA Because our department offers a more flexible curriculum, many seniors start businesses during their student years. There are also numerous students who intern in various industries, and many participate in the department’s mentorship program. Therefore, NTU BA is very suitable for students who want to explore their future paths.
Summary Freedom, diversity, and exploration are the feelings I’ve experienced in NTU BA. I hope this article can help satisfy the curiosity of those interested in NTU BA.
Disclaimer: The viewpoints in this article are solely based on the author’s personal experiences and do not represent the official stance of the department.
title: Introduction to NTU BA
